proposals.title: Temporal and Structural GFlowNets: Learning Representations for Non-i.i.d. Data via Sequential Generative Flow
proposals.motivation: Most representation learning frameworks assume i.i.d. data, which is a poor fit for the structured dependencies inherent in temporal sequences, graphs, and other relational data prevalent in scientific domains like high-energy physics and combinatorial optimization. This limitation hinders the ability to learn rich, structured representations that capture the underlying generative process of the data. Developing methods that explicitly model these dependencies is crucial for advancing probabilistic modeling in complex, real-world systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly incorporating temporal and structural constraints into the state space and flow dynamics of GFlowNets, we can learn more accurate and generalizable representations for non-i.i.d. data than methods assuming independence. This will enable the learning of distributions over sequences and graphs that respect their inherent dependencies.
proposals.method: We will extend the GFlowNet framework by defining a non-Markovian state transition process where the action space and flow constraints are conditioned on the history of the generative trajectory (for sequences) or the local graph structure (for networked data). Specifically, we will develop a Temporal GFlowNet using recurrent or transformer-based flow approximators to model sequences, and a Structural GFlowNet where the flow is parameterized by graph neural networks. Training will leverage the existing trajectory balance or detailed balance objectives, but with modified state representations that encode the dependency structure, enabling the model to learn the compositional generation of dependent data points.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled generative framework for representation learning on non-i.i.d. data, bridging GFlowNets with structured probabilistic modeling. It will provide new algorithmic tools for tasks like temporal forecasting and graph generation, directly applicable to the researcher's interests in high-energy physics and combinatorial optimization. The project will also yield insights into how generative flow-based training can be adapted to complex, constrained state spaces.
proposals.novelty: While GFlowNets have been applied to graph problems, their adaptation to explicitly model the non-i.i.d. nature of data generation processes—such as temporal causality or network autocorrelation—for general representation learning is novel. This moves beyond using GFlowNets as solvers for fixed objectives to using them as core models for learning the underlying distribution of structured data.

proposals.title: Supervised Bootstrapping for Unsupervised Energy-Based Representation Learning with GFlowNets
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in representation learning is to first learn generic features via unsupervised methods (e.g., contrastive learning, autoencoders) before fine-tuning on supervised tasks. This can be inefficient if the downstream task is known, as the unsupervised phase may not discover task-relevant structures. Conversely, purely supervised learning often yields representations that lack robustness and generalizability beyond the labeled data. A principled method to use task-specific supervision to guide the discovery of broader, generative representations remains an open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: Supervised learning signals can be used to structure the training of a generative, energy-based model, enabling it to learn richer and more generalizable unsupervised representations than those learned from a purely unsupervised or purely supervised starting point.
proposals.method: We propose a novel training framework where a GFlowNet is trained as a generative model of data representations. The GFlowNet's state space is a structured latent space, and its reward function is defined by the performance of a simple downstream predictor on a small, labeled dataset. By training the GFlowNet to sample representations proportional to this reward, it learns a generative process that prioritizes task-relevant features. This model is then used as a prior or a proposal distribution for training a broader energy-based model (EBM) on the full, unlabeled dataset, where the EBM's energy function is refined to match the GFlowNet's distribution while also modeling the full data manifold.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete algorithmic framework for inverting the unsupervised-before-supervised paradigm, demonstrating that task-specific signals can bootstrap more powerful generative models. It will contribute a new hybrid training method for EBMs and GFlowNets, evaluated on benchmarks from high-energy physics and combinatorial graph problems, showing gains in sample efficiency and out-of-distribution generalization.
proposals.novelty: While GFlowNets have been used for combinatorial optimization and generative modeling, using them as a supervised, reward-driven prior to guide the training of a broader unsupervised EBM is a novel synthesis. This inverts the typical use of unsupervised learning as a regularizer for supervised tasks.

proposals.title: Hierarchical GFlowNets: Unsupervised Discovery of Abstraction Lattices via Energy-Based Compositional Flows
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in unsupervised representation learning is discovering meaningful, multi-level abstractions from raw data without labels. Pre-2026 methods like VAEs and diffusion models often produce flat or entangled latent spaces, lacking explicit compositional and hierarchical structure. Learning such structure is crucial for sample-efficient reasoning and generalization in complex domains like high-energy physics or molecular design.
proposals.hypothesis: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets), trained to sample compositional objects proportional to a reward, can be adapted to an unsupervised, energy-based setting to discover a lattice of hierarchical abstractions, where higher-level representations correspond to more abstract, reusable components of the data.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel GFlowNet architecture where the state space is a lattice of partial abstractions, and actions correspond to composing or abstracting simpler concepts. The training objective will be based on a variational energy function, derived from a reconstruction model, that rewards representations for compressing the data while remaining predictive. We will leverage the pre-2026 Distributional GFlowNet framework to model uncertainty over abstraction levels and employ a training-by-energy-matching scheme, akin to the energy-based models in Courville's prior work, to learn the compositional flows without external reward.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, flow-based framework for unsupervised hierarchical representation learning, yielding explicit, interpretable abstraction trees or lattices. It will extend the application domain of GFlowNets from discrete combinatorial optimization to general unsupervised learning, demonstrating their utility for discovering structure in complex, high-dimensional data.
proposals.novelty: While GFlowNets have been used for structured generation with a given reward, their adaptation to *discover* the hierarchical reward structure itself from unlabeled data via energy-based learning is a novel direction. This combines the compositional sampling of GFlowNets with the flexible density estimation of energy-based models in a uniquely integrated framework.

proposals.title: Spectral Energy Guidance: Improving GFlowNet Training and Sampling via Manifold-Aware Regularization
proposals.motivation: Deep generative models, including GFlowNets, often learn representations that fail to capture the underlying topological and geometric structure of complex data manifolds, leading to inefficient exploration and mode collapse. Spectral methods from manifold learning provide powerful tools to characterize data geometry but are rarely integrated into the training dynamics of modern deep generative frameworks. This gap limits the ability of models like GFlowNets to generate diverse, high-quality samples in structured spaces, a core challenge in pre-2026 generative modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating spectral constraints derived from on-the-fly estimates of the data manifold into the GFlowNet training objective will regularize the learned state representations and policy, leading to more efficient exploration, improved mode coverage, and higher-quality sample diversity.
proposals.method: We propose to augment the GFlowNet training loss with a spectral regularization term. During training, we will maintain a running k-nearest neighbor graph in the learned representation space (e.g., the state embedding). The graph Laplacian of this construction will be computed, and its spectral properties (e.g., eigenvectors corresponding to small eigenvalues) will be used to define a penalty that encourages the GFlowNet's transition policy to align with the inferred manifold structure. This method will be integrated into the trajectory balance or detailed balance loss, tested first on standard synthetic combinatorial spaces and then on high-energy physics datasets to structure the generation of particle collision events.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel training framework that marries spectral geometry with flow-based generative training, directly addressing exploration and diversity in GFlowNets. It will provide new theoretical insights into the geometry of learned GFlowNet policies and demonstrate practical gains on benchmark tasks, including graph generation and scientific simulation. The resulting 'spectral energy' model will be a step towards more geometrically-aware generative agents.
proposals.novelty: While spectral methods are established in unsupervised learning, their integration as a dynamic, train-time regularizer for sequential generative models like GFlowNets is unexplored. The novelty lies in using the *evolving* data manifold of the GFlowNet's own samples to guide its policy learning, creating a feedback loop between representation and generation.

proposals.title: Distributed Training of Generative Flow Networks for Scalable Unsupervised Learning
proposals.motivation: Training large-scale generative models on complex, high-dimensional data (e.g., in high-energy physics or combinatorial spaces) is computationally prohibitive on single machines. Current GFlowNet training, while promising for structured and discrete domains, lacks efficient distributed frameworks, limiting model scale and data throughput. Scaling these energy-based probabilistic models is crucial for tackling real-world scientific and combinatorial problems where capturing intricate distributions is key.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by developing a novel distributed training framework specifically tailored for the sequential, off-policy nature of GFlowNets, we can achieve near-linear scaling in training efficiency, enabling the training of massively larger generative models on distributed compute clusters without sacrificing the diversity or mode-covering properties central to GFlowNets.
proposals.method: We will design a distributed GFlowNet framework using synchronized parameter servers and asynchronous actors for trajectory sampling. The core innovation is a prioritized replay system for sub-trajectories that accounts for flow matching errors, enabling efficient global credit assignment across workers. We will integrate this with quantile-based temporal difference learning for distributional GFlowNets to stabilize training. The framework will be evaluated on scaling up existing GFlowNet applications, such as graph combinatorial optimization and high-energy physics event generation, using hundreds of parallel workers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-source, production-ready framework for distributed GFlowNet training, significantly lowering the barrier to large-scale generative modeling in structured domains. It will demonstrate empirical scaling laws for GFlowNets and enable new applications requiring models of unprecedented size and complexity, directly advancing scalable unsupervised learning.
proposals.novelty: While distributed training for LLMs is well-studied, adapting these principles to the unique trajectory-based, flow-matching objective of GFlowNets—requiring careful management of off-policy samples and global flow consistency—is a novel and non-trivial challenge not addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Evaluating Latent Representations via Generative Fidelity: A GFlowNet-Energy-Based Model Framework
proposals.motivation: A core goal of representation learning is to capture the underlying data-generating process. However, standard evaluation metrics like downstream task performance are indirect and can be satisfied by representations that discard semantically important but statistically subtle details. A more direct test is to invert the learned representation mapping to generate high-fidelity data, a challenge where current generative models often fail to preserve complex, high-dimensional structure. This is especially critical in domains like high-energy physics, where simulation fidelity is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the generative fidelity of a learned representation—measured by the ability of a model conditioned on that representation to reconstruct or sample high-quality data—is a robust and necessary metric for representation quality, and that a framework combining the exploratory sampling of GFlowNets with the fine-grained density modeling of Energy-Based Models (EBMs) can effectively perform this inversion.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, a representation encoder (e.g., a transformer or CNN) maps data to a latent space. Second, a conditional generative model, trained to invert this mapping, samples data given a latent code. This generator will be a novel hybrid: a GFlowNet, leveraging its strength in diverse structured generation, will propose candidate samples, which will then be refined by a conditional EBM, using its ability to model complex, high-dimensional distributions. Training will involve a joint objective, aligning the GFlowNet's flow with the EBM's energy landscape and enforcing reconstruction consistency via the encoder.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, task-agnostic evaluation paradigm for representation learning based on generative fidelity. It will also contribute a new hybrid generative architecture that marries the exploratory benefits of GFlowNets with the precise density control of EBMs, demonstrated on complex datasets from domains like particle physics and molecular graphs.
proposals.novelty: While GFlowNets have been used for generation and EBMs for density estimation, their integration for the specific purpose of inverting and evaluating fixed representations is novel. The proposal reframes representation evaluation as a conditional generative modeling problem, moving beyond discriminative proxies.

proposals.title: Single-Object Generative Modeling via Energy-Guided GFlowNet Exploration
proposals.motivation: Generative models excel at capturing population-level distributions but struggle to learn rich, multi-faceted representations of a single, complex instance (e.g., a specific protein, a unique galaxy, or a detailed 3D asset). Pre-2026, methods for single-instance modeling are limited, often relying on handcrafted priors or inefficient search. This work addresses the fundamental challenge of building a comprehensive probabilistic model from a single, highly structured data point, which is crucial for scientific analysis and creative design where unique objects are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: A generative flow network (GFlowNet), guided by a learned energy function that scores partial constructions, can efficiently explore the vast combinatorial space of possible representations for a single object and learn a structured, generative model over its plausible variations and underlying factors.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-network framework. First, an energy model (EBM) is trained via contrastive divergence to score the 'completeness' or 'plausibility' of any partial or complete representation of the object (e.g., a subgraph, a partial parse tree, or a scene component). Second, a GFlowNet is trained to sample sequences of constructive actions (e.g., adding a component) terminating in full representations, using the negative energy as a reward. The GFlowNet's training objective (e.g., flow matching or detailed balance) ensures diverse sampling proportional to the exponentiated reward, enabling efficient exploration of the high-dimensional construction space for the single instance.
proposals.contribution: This will introduce a novel paradigm for single-object generative modeling, providing a tool for systematic exploration and manipulation of complex individual entities. It will extend the application of GFlowNets from learning distributions over datasets to learning distributions over the representational space of one datum, and demonstrate how energy-based models can provide the necessary guidance for such focused exploration.
proposals.novelty: While GFlowNets are established for learning generative policies over datasets, applying them to model the latent constructive space of a *single* instance is novel. The key innovation is using a learned energy function specific to one object as the reward for a generative construction process, marrying the representation-learning strength of EBMs with the exploratory sampling of GFlowNets.

proposals.title: Scalable Energy-Based Representation Learning for Ultra-High-Dimensional Data with Sparse GFlowNets
proposals.motivation: Modern scientific domains, such as high-energy physics with its complex detector readouts, generate data with millions of raw dimensions, posing a fundamental challenge for probabilistic modeling and representation learning. Pre-2026 methods, including standard GFlowNets and deep energy-based models, struggle with computational and statistical efficiency in these ultra-high-dimensional spaces, limiting their ability to learn compact, meaningful representations for downstream tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating explicit sparsity-inducing mechanisms and hierarchical latent variable structures into the GFlowNet training framework, we can learn energy-based models that are both scalable to millions of dimensions and capable of discovering disentangled, low-dimensional representations directly from raw data.
proposals.method: We will develop Sparse Hierarchical GFlowNets (SH-GFlowNets), building on the pre-2026 GFlowNet framework. The method employs a factorized, sequential generation process over input dimensions, guided by a sparsity-promoting prior (e.g., L0 regularization or a spike-and-slab distribution) within the state transition policy. A multi-scale energy model, parameterized by a deep neural network with efficient attention or convolutional blocks, will score partial states. Training will utilize the trajectory balance or detailed balance loss, augmented with a sparsity penalty, on data from domains like particle physics detector simulations or hyper-spectral imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable framework for energy-based representation learning in ultra-high-dimensional spaces, demonstrated on real-world scientific datasets. It will advance the theoretical understanding of training generative models over combinatorial, sparse structures and provide practical tools for data compression and feature discovery in fields previously dominated by hand-engineered features.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the principled integration of explicit, trainable sparsity within the GFlowNet sampling process for representation learning, a departure from prior work that focused on relatively lower-dimensional combinatorial objects or did not explicitly target the ultra-high-dimensional, continuous-input setting with an energy-based lens.

proposals.title: Adversarial Anatomical Priors for Segmentation: A GFlowNet Energy-Based Training Framework
proposals.motivation: Deep learning-based medical image segmentation often produces anatomically implausible results, violating constraints on shape, connectivity, and topology. This limits clinical trust and downstream utility. While adversarial training in generative models like GANs enforces realism in synthesized data, this powerful concept remains underexplored for directly imposing anatomical consistency in discriminative segmentation tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adversarial training framework, where a segmentation network is trained against a critic network that scores anatomical realism, can be effectively structured using GFlowNets and energy-based models to enforce hard constraints and learn complex, multi-modal distributions of valid anatomical segmentations.
proposals.method: We will develop a GFlowNet-based adversarial training loop. The segmentor (a UNet) generates candidate masks, which are evaluated by a pre-trained or jointly trained Energy-Based Model (EBM) acting as the adversarial critic, defining an energy landscape where low energy corresponds to anatomically plausible configurations. A GFlowNet will be trained to sample segmentations proportional to exp(-E(mask)), providing a diverse set of 'corrected' examples. These samples will be used to adversarially train the segmentor via a loss that combines task fidelity (e.g., Dice) with a term minimizing the energy of its outputs, as judged by the EBM critic.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel training paradigm that integrates adversarial learning, EBMs, and GFlowNets to inject hard anatomical knowledge into segmentation networks. We will provide a principled method to improve segmentation robustness and plausibility, demonstrated on public 3D medical imaging datasets (e.g., BraTS, MSD). The framework will advance the use of generative principles for structured discriminative tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of GFlowNets—excellent for sampling from complex, discrete distributions—with an EBM-based adversarial critic to guide segmentation training, moving beyond standard GAN-style adversaries. This directly connects Courville's expertise in GFlowNets and EBMs to the core problem of anatomical constraint enforcement.

proposals.title: Dual-Objective GFlowNets for Bidirectional Vision-Language Alignment
proposals.motivation: Learning robust, aligned representations between images and text remains a core challenge in multimodal AI. Pre-2026, methods often train separate, unidirectional models (e.g., CLIP-style contrastive or autoregressive captioners), which can lead to representation gaps and poor sample efficiency. A unified probabilistic framework that explicitly models the bidirectional conditional distributions P(caption|image) and P(image|caption) could yield more coherent and informative joint representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a single GFlowNet as a stochastic policy for both image-to-caption and caption-to-image generation within a shared energy-based model will enforce stronger consistency in the learned latent space, leading to improved sample efficiency and bidirectional inference quality compared to unidirectional or disjointly trained models.
proposals.method: We propose a novel Dual-Objective GFlowNet (DO-GFN) architecture. A shared energy function, parameterized by a neural network, will define E(image, caption). The DO-GFN will learn two separate but coupled flow networks: one that samples captions given an image (a trajectory in text space), and another that samples image features or patches given a caption (a trajectory in image space). Training will use a modified trajectory balance loss that jointly minimizes discrepancies for both directional policies, regularized by the consistency of the shared energy. We will leverage pre-trained vision and language encoders for feature extraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel GFlowNet formulation for bidirectional conditional modeling, expanding the application of GFlowNets beyond unidirectional generation. It will provide empirical evidence on datasets like COCO and Conceptual Captions that joint bidirectional training improves metrics for both image captioning and text-to-image retrieval/reconstruction compared to strong baselines, advancing energy-based multimodal learning.
proposals.novelty: While GFlowNets have been applied to sequence generation and combinatorial problems, their application to learn a single model for two coupled, stochastic conditional policies is novel. The core innovation is the shared energy-based training that enforces bidirectional consistency, moving beyond prior dual-learning setups that often use separate, deterministically linked models.

proposals.title: Memorization as a Generative Bottleneck: Energy-Based Analysis and Training with GFlowNets in Severely Underparameterized Regimes
proposals.motivation: Understanding the interplay between memorization and generalization is a core, unresolved problem in deep learning. Prior work on grokking and double descent focuses on overparameterized models, leaving the dynamics of severely underparameterized networks—where even fitting the training set requires memorization—largely unexplored. This regime is critical for understanding fundamental model capacity limits and has practical implications for efficient, low-resource deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in severely underparameterized regimes, the training dynamics and final solutions found by generative models are fundamentally governed by the competition to memorize distinct training examples, and that GFlowNets, due to their compositional generative process, will exhibit qualitatively different and more structured memorization patterns compared to standard likelihood-based models.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled experimental framework using low-capacity energy-based models (EBMs) and GFlowNets on standard image datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10). Model capacity will be drastically scaled down by limiting network width/depth. We will analyze memorization via metrics like per-example loss trajectories, influence functions, and output diversity. A key technical component will be adapting the GFlowNet training objective—specifically the Detailed Balance loss—to this high-pressure memorization setting, comparing its discovered solution(s) to those found by contrastive divergence (CD) or score matching for EBMs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of memorization in the severely underparameterized regime for modern generative models. It will yield new insights into the inductive biases of GFlowNets versus EBMs under extreme capacity constraints and may lead to novel principles for designing compact, high-fidelity models.
proposals.novelty: While memorization is well-studied in large models, the deliberate study of it as a necessary, dominant force in tiny models is novel. Combining this with a comparative analysis of GFlowNets and EBMs bridges the themes of energy-based learning and generative flow networks in an unexplored, constrained regime.

proposals.title: Inverse Diffusion via GFlowNets: Probabilistic Extraction of Memorized Noise from Trained Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Modern diffusion models and other deep generative networks are known to memorize and regurgitate training data, raising significant privacy and copyright concerns. Current membership inference and data extraction attacks are often deterministic or heuristic, lacking a principled probabilistic framework to model the space of possible memorized noise latents. A formal, generative approach to this inverse problem is needed to better understand and quantify memorization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the space of noise latents that can be inverted to reconstruct specific training data from a fixed model forms a structured, learnable distribution. This distribution can be modeled and sampled from using generative flow networks, providing a more complete picture of memorization than single-point estimates.
proposals.method: We will frame the extraction of memorized noise as a probabilistic inverse problem. Given a trained diffusion model and a target training sample, we will train a GFlowNet to sample from the posterior distribution over initial noise vectors that reconstruct the target. The GFlowNet's state will be the partially constructed noise tensor, with actions adding noise dimensions. The reward will be based on the reconstruction fidelity (e.g., LPIPS, L2) of the generated image, encouraging the flow to discover high-likelihood noise configurations. We will leverage energy-based formulations from Courville's prior work to shape the reward and exploration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, probabilistic framework for auditing model memorization, moving beyond binary membership inference. It will deliver a practical tool (GFlowNet-based sampler) for extracting the distribution of memorized latents. The findings will advance the understanding of the geometry of the inverse mapping in diffusion models and contribute to the development of more privacy-preserving training algorithms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has attempted to find a single noise seed, our approach is the first to model the entire *distribution* of memorized noise using GFlowNets. This connects the fields of generative model auditing and generative flow networks in a novel way, applying GFlowNets to an inverse inference task rather than forward generation.

proposals.title: The Memorization-Simplification Frontier in Generative Models: A GFlowNet and Energy-Based Investigation
proposals.motivation: Recent work in supervised learning has identified a fundamental trade-off between a model's ability to memorize training data and its capacity for generalization (simplification). It is unknown if and how this trade-off manifests in deep generative models, which are central to modern AI. Understanding this frontier is critical for diagnosing overfitting, improving sample efficiency, and ensuring models learn robust distributions rather than replicating artifacts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a similar memorization-simplification frontier exists for generative models, where increased model capacity and training compute can lead to a sharp transition from learning a smoothed data distribution to memorizing individual training examples. Furthermore, we posit that GFlowNets, due to their compositional generative process and explicit training towards a target distribution, may exhibit a different and potentially more favorable frontier compared to implicit models like GANs.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled empirical study using synthetic and real-world datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, a particle physics dataset). We will train a suite of models—including GANs, Energy-Based Models (EBMs), and GFlowNets—across a spectrum of capacities and training durations. Memorization will be quantified using precision/recall metrics against the training set, nearest neighbor analysis of generated samples, and influence functions. Simplification/generalization will be measured via FID, diversity metrics on held-out data, and performance on downstream tasks enabled by the generative model, such as providing rewards for a GFlowNet policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic characterization of the memorization-simplification trade-off in generative modeling. It will yield practical guidelines for diagnosing overfitting in generative training. By comparing paradigms, it will clarify the relative strengths of flow-based, energy-based, and adversarial approaches in navigating this frontier.
proposals.novelty: While memorization is studied in discriminative models, its systematic analysis in generative models is nascent. The direct comparison of GFlowNets—which learn to sample from a *reward* distribution—against traditional likelihood-based and implicit models on this axis is novel, leveraging Courville's expertise in unifying these frameworks.

proposals.title: GFlow-Energy Guided Adversarial Training: Stabilizing and Scaling GANs for High-Resolution Data
proposals.motivation: Training Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) on high-resolution or complex 3D data remains challenging due to unstable training dynamics, mode collapse, and prohibitive computational costs. These limitations hinder their application in scientific domains like high-energy physics simulation and medical imaging, where high-fidelity, diverse samples are crucial. A principled framework to guide the adversarial training process towards better exploration of the data distribution is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a GFlowNet as an auxiliary exploration policy within the GAN framework can stabilize training and improve sample diversity by explicitly modeling the generative process as a flow of states, thereby mitigating mode collapse and enabling more efficient scaling to high-resolution data.
proposals.method: We propose a novel hybrid architecture where a GFlowNet, trained to sample trajectories in a learned latent space or a feature space of a pre-trained encoder, provides intermediate rewards or state proposals to the GAN generator. The generator is trained not only via adversarial loss but also to maximize the likelihood of trajectories proposed by the GFlowNet, which is itself trained on successful (high-discriminator-score) generated samples. We will leverage energy-based formulations from Courville's prior work to define the reward for the GFlowNet, linking it to the discriminator's confidence. Techniques like progressive growing and multi-scale discriminators will be integrated for high-resolution scalability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, stable training algorithm for high-resolution GANs, demonstrated on benchmarks like FFHQ (1024x1024) and complex 3D object datasets. It will provide a theoretical bridge between adversarial training and flow-based probabilistic modeling, showing how structured exploration can alleviate fundamental GAN failures. The released code and models will benefit applications requiring high-fidelity generative models.
proposals.novelty: While GFlowNets have been used for combinatorial optimization and reinforcement learning, their integration as an intrinsic guide for GAN training is unexplored. This moves beyond using GFlowNets as standalone generative models, instead leveraging their strength in exploring compositional spaces to directly stabilize another generative framework.

proposals.title: Bayesian Optimization of Generative Flow Network Architectures for Energy-Based Learning
proposals.motivation: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) have emerged as a powerful framework for learning to sample from complex, high-dimensional distributions, particularly in structured spaces like molecules and graphs. However, their performance is highly sensitive to architectural choices and hyperparameters, such as the flow matching objective and network depth. Manual tuning is inefficient and suboptimal, hindering their application to new domains. A systematic, sample-efficient optimization method is needed to unlock their full potential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian optimization (BO) framework, leveraging a probabilistic surrogate model and an acquisition function tailored for GFlowNet training dynamics, can efficiently discover high-performing hyperparameter and architectural configurations, leading to more stable training and improved sample diversity compared to standard grid or random search.
proposals.method: We will develop a BO loop where the objective function is the performance (e.g., sample diversity, target distribution fidelity) of a GFlowNet trained on a benchmark task like molecule generation. The surrogate model will be a Gaussian Process (GP) regressor, modeling the performance landscape over a joint space of continuous (e.g., learning rate, entropy coefficient) and categorical (e.g., choice of flow loss, network type) parameters. We will employ an acquisition function, such as Expected Improvement, to guide the search. The framework will be validated on standard pre-2026 GFlowNet benchmarks and a high-energy physics dataset to demonstrate robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, automated methodology for configuring GFlowNets, reducing the practitioner burden and improving reproducibility. It will yield new insights into the relationship between GFlowNet architecture, training stability, and final performance. The resulting open-source optimization tool will accelerate research and deployment of GFlowNets in scientific domains.
proposals.novelty: While BO has been used for tuning deep neural networks, its application to the unique training dynamics and multi-objective (flow matching) loss landscape of GFlowNets is novel. This approach moves beyond tuning simple GANs, focusing instead on the more structured and sequential generation process of GFlowNets, where sample efficiency during hyperparameter search is paramount.

proposals.title: Generative Flow Networks for Continual Learning: A GFlowNet-Based Framework for Catastrophic Forgetting-Free GANs
proposals.motivation: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are powerful but suffer from catastrophic forgetting when trained on non-stationary data streams, limiting their application in real-world, evolving environments. Pre-2026, continual learning for GANs remains a significant challenge, with existing replay- or regularization-based methods often compromising stability or plasticity. This work addresses the core need for generative models that can learn new data distributions sequentially without forgetting previous ones.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing the sequential generation of tasks as a constructive, stochastic process and training a GFlowNet to sample from a sequence of energy-based models (EBMs) representing each task will enable a single generator to learn continuously without catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.method: We propose to model each data distribution (task) with a distinct EBM, defining a composite energy landscape for the sequence of tasks. A single GFlowNet generator will be trained to sample from this composite distribution using a modified trajectory balance loss. The GFlowNet's inherent preference for diverse, mode-covering exploration, combined with a rehearsal buffer storing trajectories (partial samples) from previous tasks' EBMs, will enforce coverage of all learned distributions. We will adapt the training framework from 'Versatile Energy-Based Probabilistic Models for High Energy Physics' and the quantile flows from 'Distributional GFlowNets' to stabilize multi-distribution learning.
proposals.contribution: This will yield the first GFlowNet-based framework for continual learning in generative modeling, providing a principled solution to catastrophic forgetting in GANs. It will advance the field by demonstrating how generative flow networks can unify sequential decision-making and incremental distribution learning, offering a new paradigm beyond adversarial training for continual learning.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its core formulation: it replaces the adversarial GAN objective with a GFlowNet trained on a sequence of EBMs, directly tackling forgetting through flow matching rather than parameter regularization. This leverages Courville's expertise in energy-based models and GFlowNets to solve a longstanding problem in generative modeling from a unique, non-adversarial perspective.

proposals.title: Amortized Posterior Inference for Generative Models via Energy-Guided GFlowNets
proposals.motivation: Accurate inference of latent codes from observed data is a critical inverse problem for controllable generation, data editing, and likelihood-free inference in complex generative models. Current methods, such as variational inference or iterative optimization, are often inefficient, biased, or struggle with multi-modal posterior distributions. This bottleneck limits the practical utility of powerful generative frameworks in scientific domains like high-energy physics, where understanding the latent causes of observations is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing amortized posterior inference as a sequential sampling problem, solved by GFlowNets, can produce a more expressive and efficient inference network capable of capturing complex, multi-modal posterior distributions over latent codes.
proposals.method: We propose to train a GFlowNet to sample trajectories that construct latent codes conditioned on an observed data point. The reward function will be defined by the generator's likelihood (or energy) and a prior, guiding the sampler toward high-probability regions of the true posterior. We will integrate this with the energy-based modeling expertise from the researcher's prior work, potentially using a quantile-based parametrization for the flow to improve training stability and distributional coverage. The inference GFlowNet will be trained jointly or alternatively with the generator to ensure consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, principled framework for amortized posterior inference that scales to high-dimensional latent spaces and captures posterior multimodality. It will extend the application of GFlowNets from generative modeling to inverse problems, providing a powerful tool for analysis and manipulation within trained generative models, with direct applications in scientific simulation-based inference.
proposals.novelty: While GFlowNets have been used for forward generative modeling, their application to the inverse problem of inference, guided by a generator's energy function, is novel. This approach moves beyond point estimates or simple variational families to sample from the full posterior distribution efficiently.

proposals.title: GFlowNet-Guided Training for Scalable Conditional Computation in Large Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Conditional computation, where parts of a model are dynamically activated via learned gating, promises dramatic gains in efficiency for large-scale models. However, scaling these methods is hindered by the challenge of training discrete, stochastic gating units with non-differentiable objectives. Pre-2026, methods like REINFORCE and Gumbel-Softmax face high-variance gradient estimates or bias, limiting their application to networks with many gates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing the training of large-scale conditional computation models as a sequential sampling problem and applying GFlowNet training objectives will provide lower-variance, more stable gradient estimates than existing methods, enabling effective scaling.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel training framework where the sequential activation of model components (e.g., experts in a Mixture-of-Experts layer) is modeled as a trajectory sampled by a GFlowNet. The GFlowNet's policy, parameterized alongside the main model weights, will learn to generate subsets of active units with probability proportional to a reward defined by the training objective (e.g., loss reduction). We will leverage pre-2026 advances in Distributional GFlowNets and quantile flows to stabilize training and integrate energy-based modeling perspectives to shape the reward landscape, applying the method to large-scale vision and language models.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, principled algorithm for training large models with conditional computation, demonstrated on benchmarks with thousands of gating decisions. It will advance the scalability of efficient, sparse models and contribute a novel application of GFlowNets beyond discrete structure generation to the core problem of dynamic neural network training.
proposals.novelty: While GFlowNets have been applied to combinatorial optimization, their use for direct, gradient-based training of the stochastic gates within a large, end-to-end differentiable model is novel. This bridges Courville's expertise in GFlowNets and energy-based models with the practical challenge of scaling conditional computation.

proposals.title: Gradient Estimator Performance in Deep Stochastic Networks: A GFlowNet and Energy-Based Model Benchmark
proposals.motivation: Training deep generative models with multiple stochastic layers remains a significant challenge due to the difficulty of propagating gradients through discrete or structured latent variables. While estimators like REINFORCE, Gumbel-Softmax, and Concrete relaxations are well-studied in shallow settings, their comparative performance and stability in deeper architectures—critical for scaling models like GFlowNets and Energy-Based Models (EBMs) to complex, structured outputs—are poorly understood. This gap hinders progress in applying these powerful probabilistic frameworks to high-dimensional combinatorial spaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in deep stochastic networks, gradient estimators based on path-wise derivatives (e.g., Gumbel-Softmax) will exhibit lower variance but increased bias in deeper layers, while score-function estimators (e.g., REINFORCE) will suffer from variance that compounds with depth, and that GFlowNet-specific training objectives may offer a more stable alternative for certain multi-layer stochastic structures.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled benchmark of multi-layer stochastic networks, where each layer samples from a categorical distribution parameterized by the previous layer's output. We will implement and compare standard estimators (REINFORCE, NVIL, Gumbel-Softmax), GFlowNet-specific estimators (Trajectory Balance, Detailed Balance), and hybrid energy-based methods. The architectures will be trained on synthetic structured prediction tasks and scaled graph combinatorial problems. Performance will be evaluated on convergence speed, solution quality, gradient variance per layer, and sample efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic empirical analysis of gradient estimators in deep stochastic networks, yielding practical guidelines for architecting and training deep GFlowNets and EBMs. It will identify failure modes and optimal estimator choices for depth, directly advancing the scalability of these models for high-energy physics and combinatorial optimization applications.
proposals.novelty: While gradient estimators are widely analyzed, a controlled, depth-focused benchmark within the specific context of GFlowNet and EBM training frameworks is novel. The investigation of how the compositional structure of GFlowNet trajectories interacts with estimator choice in multi-layer networks is an unexplored and critical question for scaling.

proposals.title: Stable Policy Gradient Training for Generative Flow Networks
proposals.motivation: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) have emerged as a powerful framework for learning to sample from complex, high-dimensional distributions, with applications in combinatorial optimization and scientific discovery. However, their training, often based on flow-matching objectives, can suffer from high variance and instability, especially in sparse-reward or stochastic environments. Advanced policy gradient methods from deep reinforcement learning (RL), such as PPO and TRPO, have been extensively developed to address precisely these issues of stability and sample efficiency, but their adaptation to the GFlowNet paradigm remains largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting modern policy gradient techniques, specifically those designed for variance reduction and trust region optimization, to the GFlowNet training objective will yield more stable, sample-efficient, and scalable learning of generative policies over compositional spaces.
proposals.method: We will formulate the GFlowNet generative policy as a stochastic policy in an RL context, where the state is the partial object (e.g., a partial graph) and actions are construction steps. We will derive policy gradient estimators for core GFlowNet objectives (e.g., trajectory balance) and integrate advanced RL techniques: 1) Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE) for lower-variance credit assignment, 2) a clipped surrogate objective (inspired by PPO) to prevent destructive policy updates, and 3) an entropy bonus to encourage exploration. We will benchmark this approach against standard GFlowNet training on established tasks like molecule generation and graph combinatorial optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled methodology for stabilizing GFlowNet training, potentially accelerating convergence and improving final sample diversity. It will establish a stronger formal and practical bridge between RL policy optimization and generative flow networks, enriching the toolkit for probabilistic modeling over discrete structures. The resulting algorithms will be validated on high-energy physics and combinatorial optimization benchmarks from prior work.
proposals.novelty: While GFlowNets are inspired by RL, the direct application of state-of-the-art policy gradient *optimization techniques* (beyond simple REINFORCE) to their training loop is novel. This moves beyond the analogy to develop a hybrid training framework that leverages the strengths of both fields.

proposals.title: Inverting Stochastic Generators: Bayesian Input Inference for GFlowNets and Energy-Based Models
proposals.motivation: Stochastic generative models like GFlowNets and Energy-Based Models (EBMs) are powerful for sampling complex, structured outputs. However, a critical inverse problem remains underexplored: given an observed output (e.g., a molecule, a graph, or a particle jet), what is the most likely input (e.g., a latent code or initial state) that generated it? Solving this is essential for interpretability, model-based diagnosis, and tasks like experimental design in scientific domains such as high-energy physics, where one infers underlying parameters from stochastic detector outputs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the training dynamics and learned state flows of a GFlowNet, or the energy function of an EBM, provide sufficient structure to formulate and efficiently solve a tractable Bayesian inverse problem for input inference, outperforming generic gradient-based inversion methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a unified framework for input inference by treating the trained stochastic network as a conditional likelihood model. For GFlowNets, we will leverage the learned flow functions to define a posterior over trajectories and inputs, using the detailed balance or trajectory balance constraints to guide MCMC or variational inference. For EBMs, we will employ Langevin dynamics within a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) scheme that samples from the posterior over inputs given the observed output and the model's energy landscape. We will demonstrate the approach on combinatorial graph generation and high-energy physics simulation tasks from Courville's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic method for inverting GFlowNets, extending their utility beyond generation to analysis and inference. It will yield a novel tool for scientific discovery, such as inferring particle collision parameters from detector data, and advance the theoretical understanding of inverse problems in modern stochastic generative frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While input inference is studied for deterministic networks, the structured stochasticity and compositional generation of GFlowNets present a distinct challenge. Our approach is novel in directly exploiting the flow-matching constraints of GFlowNets for inversion, a connection not made in prior work on EBMs or standard generative models.

proposals.title: Spectral Bias in Convolutional Networks: A GFlowNet-Based Analysis of Inductive Biases in Frequency Learning
proposals.motivation: Spectral bias, the tendency of neural networks to learn low-frequency functions faster, is well-documented in fully-connected networks but poorly understood in convolutional architectures. Given the central role of CNNs in computer vision and scientific domains, a precise characterization of their frequency-dependent learning dynamics is crucial for improving generalization and sample efficiency. This investigation bridges the gap between spectral learning theory and practical architectural design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the weight-sharing structure of convolutional networks induces a distinct spectral bias profile, systematically favoring the learning of specific frequency bands aligned with the spatial scale of the kernels, which differs fundamentally from the bias in fully-connected models.
proposals.method: We will employ a novel GFlowNet-based framework to sample and analyze the learning trajectories of CNNs across frequency space. By training GFlowNets to generate training datasets with controlled spectral properties, we can perform targeted experiments to measure learning speed as a function of frequency. We will compare standard CNNs with varied kernel sizes and depths against fully-connected baselines, using Fourier analysis of the network's output evolution during training on synthetic data like parameterized sinusoidal functions and natural image patches.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, empirical characterization of spectral bias in convolutional networks, linking architectural hyperparameters to frequency learning order. It will deliver a new diagnostic tool using GFlowNets for probing model biases and offer concrete guidelines for designing architectures with desired spectral properties for applications like image reconstruction or scientific data analysis.
proposals.novelty: While spectral bias has been studied, applying the generative sampling capabilities of GFlowNets to actively probe this bias in CNNs is a novel methodology. Furthermore, explicitly disentangling the effect of weight sharing from other factors on the spectral learning profile represents an unexplored direction.

proposals.title: Decoupled Adversarial Training with Black-Box Optimization and GFlowNets
proposals.motivation: Adversarial training frameworks like GANs are limited by the requirement for end-to-end differentiability, preventing the integration of non-differentiable components such as symbolic reasoners, legacy simulators, or discrete samplers. This restriction hinders the application of adversarial learning to complex scientific domains like high-energy physics, where generative models must interface with established, non-differentiable simulation code. A method to train such hybrid systems would unlock new applications for generative adversarial principles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that decoupling the generator and discriminator updates via an asynchronous, turn-based protocol using black-box optimization will lead to more stable and effective training of adversarial systems composed of arbitrary, non-differentiable programs, compared to simultaneous gradient-based approaches.
proposals.method: We propose a framework where a frozen discriminator (e.g., an energy-based model) provides a reward signal to train a generator via a black-box optimizer like CMA-ES or a GFlowNet, treating the generator as a policy for discrete or structured outputs. The roles then reverse: the frozen generator produces samples to train the discriminator via a separate black-box step. Crucially, we will instantiate the generator as a GFlowNet, leveraging its ability to sample compositional objects, and the discriminator as an energy model, aligning with Courville's expertise. The protocol iterates until convergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical framework for adversarial training with non-differentiable components, directly applicable to domains like material design or physics simulation. It will extend the versatility of GFlowNets by integrating them into a stable, adversarial learning loop with black-box optimization, providing new theoretical insights into decoupled adversarial dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit decoupling and use of black-box optimization (instead of gradient descent) for both adversarial roles within a single framework, combined with the specific integration of GFlowNets as generative policies, moving beyond differentiable neural networks.

proposals.title: Unified Energy-Directing Flows: A Single-Network Framework for Generative Training
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks (GANs) and related frameworks rely on a two-model dynamic between a generator and a discriminator/critic, which is notoriously unstable and difficult to train. This adversarial setup creates a moving target problem and mode collapse. A unified model that internalizes both generation and evaluation could lead to more stable, efficient, and theoretically coherent training paradigms, aligning with the pursuit of simpler and more robust generative learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single neural network can be trained to simultaneously represent a generative policy (a GFlowNet) and an energy function, such that the network's own energy evaluations direct its sampling policy towards low-energy (high-reward) configurations, creating a stable, self-consistent learning loop.
proposals.method: We propose a novel architecture where a single neural network outputs both a scalar energy E(x) and a stochastic policy π(x_t | x_{<t}) for constructing discrete objects (e.g., graphs, sequences). The training objective combines a GFlowNet trajectory balance loss, ensuring π samples proportionally to exp(-E(x)), with a temporal difference loss on the energy function to fit it to a target reward. Crucially, gradients from the energy fit will flow into the policy parameters, and vice-versa, within the same network, using techniques from distributional GFlowNets and energy-based model training.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, stable single-network framework for generative modeling of complex discrete structures, directly applicable to combinatorial optimization and scientific design. It will provide empirical evidence that the adversarial two-model assumption is not necessary for learning diverse, high-reward distributions, and will offer new theoretical insights into the coupling of energy and flow.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses separate networks for the flow and energy (or reward), our approach collapses them into one, creating a novel self-supervised, energy-directing flow. This differs fundamentally from prior attempts to unify GAN components, as it leverages the off-policy, compositional nature of GFlowNet training rather than adversarial min-max optimization.

proposals.title: Adversarial Latent Exploration for Mode Coverage in Generative Flow Networks
proposals.motivation: Generative models, including GFlowNets, often suffer from mode collapse, failing to capture the full diversity of complex target distributions. This is particularly limiting for scientific applications like high-energy physics, where modeling all plausible event configurations is critical. Current methods for improving coverage, such as fixed noise priors or replay buffers, are often brittle or inefficient, creating a need for more robust, learnable exploration strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that decoupling latent space exploration from sample generation via a learnable, adversarially-regularized explorer network will enable more comprehensive mode coverage in GFlowNets, leading to higher sample diversity and quality without relying on fixed input noise distributions.
proposals.method: We propose a two-component system: a latent explorer network and a primary GFlowNet generator. The explorer, a conditional stochastic policy, generates latent vectors given a learnable context variable. Its outputs are optimized via a two-pronged objective: a reward from the generator's training loss (usefulness) and an adversarial loss from a discriminator that penalizes lack of diversity in the latent batch. The generator is then trained as a standard GFlowNet on trajectories initiated from these explored latents, learning a robust mapping to the data space.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training framework for GFlowNets that explicitly optimizes for latent space diversity, directly addressing mode collapse. It will provide a flexible, learnable alternative to fixed noise priors, advancing the state-of-the-art in generative modeling for complex scientific distributions. The proposed adversarial exploration mechanism could be integrated into other energy-based or flow-based generative models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit decoupling and adversarial training of a dedicated latent exploration policy, which is a departure from using fixed noise or simple entropy bonuses. This creates a synergistic, adaptive loop between exploration and generation that is not present in prior GFlowNet or standard generative adversarial training schemes.

proposals.title: Multi-Scale Adversarial Learning via Hierarchical GFlowNet Energy Guidance
proposals.motivation: Scaling generative models to complex, high-dimensional data like high-resolution scientific simulations remains a significant challenge. GANs suffer from training instability and mode collapse, while recent energy-based models and GFlowNets offer promising alternatives for structured generation but struggle with computational efficiency for large-scale outputs. This work addresses the need for stable, scalable generative frameworks capable of capturing intricate, multi-scale dependencies prevalent in domains like high-energy physics and fluid dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical coarse-to-fine generative framework, where a cascade of GFlowNets guided by multi-scale energy functions decomposes the sampling process, will yield more stable training and higher-fidelity samples for complex data distributions than monolithic adversarial or flow-based approaches.
proposals.method: We propose a novel generative architecture where a pyramid of GFlowNets operates at successively finer resolutions. A coarse-scale GFlowNet, trained using a Temporal Difference-like objective on a low-resolution energy model, generates initial global structures. Its output seeds a finer-scale GFlowNet, which refines details using a conditional policy and a corresponding higher-resolution energy-based discriminator. This hierarchical decomposition is inspired by multi-grid PDE solvers. The energy models at each scale are trained jointly with the GFlowNets, providing a stable learning signal analogous to adversarial training but grounded in explicit likelihood-based objectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, stable framework for scalable generative modeling of complex data by fusing hierarchical adversarial concepts with the compositional sampling of GFlowNets. It will demonstrate the framework's efficacy on challenging benchmarks, including high-energy physics detector simulations and turbulent flow data, advancing the application of generative models to large-scale scientific problems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of GFlowNets into a multi-scale adversarial energy-based framework, creating a structured, coarse-to-fine generation process. This moves beyond simple progressive growing of GANs or single-scale GFlowNets by explicitly leveraging hierarchical energy decompositions for guided, tractable sampling.

proposals.title: Progressive Curriculum GANs: Stabilizing Training via a Motor Learning Analogy
proposals.motivation: Training Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) remains notoriously unstable, often suffering from mode collapse and convergence failures, especially on complex, high-dimensional datasets. While curriculum learning has been explored in supervised learning, its principled application to adversarial generative training is underdeveloped. Drawing inspiration from the structured skill acquisition in sports science offers a novel, biologically-plausible framework to address this core instability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that structuring GAN training as a curriculum of progressively more complex data distributions—analogous to motor skill progression—will significantly improve training stability and final sample quality by ensuring the generator builds robust foundational representations before tackling harder modes.
proposals.method: We will formalize a curriculum scheduler that defines a sequence of increasingly complex target distributions for the generator. Starting with low-resolution images or clustered latent embeddings, we will use a GFlowNet-based scheduler (inspired by Courville's prior work) to learn or define the optimal progression of tasks. The generator and discriminator will be trained adversarially at each stage, with the discriminator's task scaled in parallel (e.g., starting with easy real/fake discrimination on blurred data). We will test this on datasets like CIFAR-10 and ImageNet, progressively increasing resolution, class diversity, and feature complexity.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, stable training paradigm for GANs grounded in a cognitive science analogy. It will provide a practical framework for curriculum design in adversarial settings and potentially improve sample quality and mode coverage in high-dimensional generative modeling. The integration of a GFlowNet-inspired scheduler offers a principled way to automate curriculum progression.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of motor skill acquisition principles to GAN training, a connection not previously explored. While progressive growing of GANs exists, our method explicitly formulates the entire training as a learnable curriculum over data distributions, not just architectures, using energy-based concepts and scheduling inspired by GFlowNets.

proposals.title: Curriculum Learning for Generative Adversarial Networks via Adaptive Energy-Based Scheduling
proposals.motivation: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) remain notoriously difficult to train, suffering from mode collapse and instability. Curriculum learning, which gradually exposes a model to harder tasks, has proven successful in supervised and reinforcement learning but has seen limited, ad-hoc application in GANs. A principled, adaptive curriculum framework for GANs could significantly improve training robustness and sample quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum learning strategy, formalized through an adaptive energy-based schedule derived from intermediate GFlowNet states, can guide GAN training through progressively more complex data distributions, leading to more stable convergence and higher-fidelity generation.
proposals.method: We will leverage the energy-based modeling perspective from Courville's prior work to define a difficulty metric for data samples. A GFlowNet, pre-trained on a simple proxy task or low-resolution data, will be used to generate a sequence of increasingly complex training distributions. The GAN's generator and discriminator will be trained on these distributions, with the schedule adapted based on the discriminator's loss (as a proxy for task difficulty) and the diversity of generator outputs, measured using the GFlowNet's state visitation entropy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, theoretically grounded curriculum learning framework for generative modeling. It will demonstrate improved stability and sample diversity in GAN training on complex, multi-modal datasets. The integration of GFlowNets for curriculum scheduling offers a new methodological bridge between generative flows and adversarial training.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal use of GFlowNets and energy-based models to dynamically generate and schedule the training curriculum, moving beyond simple heuristics like image resolution scaling. This creates a synergistic loop where the GFlowNet structures the learning process for the GAN.

proposals.title: HyperGAN: Co-Evolving GAN Architectures and Losses via Hypernetwork-Guided Latent Space Search
proposals.motivation: Designing effective and stable Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) remains a labor-intensive process requiring expert knowledge to balance architectural choices and loss functions. Pre-2026, neural architecture search (NAS) for GANs is computationally prohibitive due to the need to train each candidate model to convergence. This bottleneck prevents the automated discovery of novel, high-performance adversarial learning frameworks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a co-evolutionary algorithm operating on compact latent vectors, which condition a shared hypernetwork to generate full generator and discriminator parameters, can efficiently and jointly discover performant GAN architectures and loss functions, overcoming the computational barriers of direct population-based NAS.
proposals.method: We will implement a co-evolutionary system with separate populations for generators (G) and discriminators (D). Each individual is represented by a low-dimensional latent code. A G-hypernetwork and a D-hypernetwork map these codes to full network weights and architectural graphs (e.g., via a DARTS-like supernet). Simultaneously, a loss function component space (e.g., mixing terms from hinge, Wasserstein, and relativistic losses) is evolved. Fitness is computed via short-term adversarial evaluation against a sample from the opposing population. The hypernetworks are trained online using gradient descent on losses from high-fitness individuals, aligning the latent space with performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, automated pipeline for GAN configuration discovery, producing novel, stable model-loss pairs. It will advance the understanding of the joint architecture-loss design space in adversarial learning. The hypernetwork-based latent evolution framework provides a new, efficient paradigm for population-based optimization in deep learning.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the drastic compression of the evolutionary search space via hypernetwork-conditioned latent codes, enabling feasible co-evolution of architectures and loss functions—a joint search not previously attempted. This merges hypernetworks, evolutionary algorithms, and adversarial training in a novel, synergistic framework.

proposals.title: Policy Gradient GANs: Training Discrete Generative Adversarial Networks via Reinforcement Learning and Energy-Based Guidance
proposals.motivation: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) struggle with discrete data generation (e.g., text, molecules, graphs) due to the non-differentiability of the sampling process, which prevents gradient backpropagation from the discriminator to the generator. Pre-2026 methods like Gumbel-Softmax or score function estimators often suffer from high variance or bias, limiting their scalability and stability. This problem is central to applying powerful GAN frameworks to critical domains like drug discovery and program synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing the discrete GAN generator as a reinforcement learning agent, optimized via policy gradient methods with a dense reward signal derived from the discriminator's energy-based output, will yield a stable and scalable training algorithm capable of generating high-quality discrete sequences and structures.
proposals.method: We will formalize the discrete generator as a stochastic policy network. The discriminator, modeled as an Energy-Based Model (EBM) as in Courville's prior work, will provide a dense reward signal: R(x) = log(D(x)) - log(1 - D(x)). We will adapt advanced policy gradient algorithms, specifically REINFORCE with baseline and Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization for variance reduction, to train the generator. The training objective will minimize the Jensen-Shannon divergence, with the policy gradient estimated via the REINFORCE rule: ∇_θ J(θ) ≈ E_{x∼G_θ}[∇_θ log G_θ(x) (R(x) - b)], where b is a learned baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, theoretically grounded framework for training GANs on discrete data, directly addressing the non-differentiability bottleneck. We will demonstrate its efficacy on benchmark tasks such as text generation (using character/word-level models) and molecular graph generation, showing improved mode coverage and training stability compared to existing discrete GAN baselines.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been used for sequence generation (e.g., SeqGAN), the novelty lies in the explicit integration of policy gradients within the standard GAN minimax framework for general discrete structures, coupled with an energy-based discriminator to provide a principled, dense reward landscape, moving beyond simple Monte Carlo rollouts.

proposals.title: Multi-Scale Adversarial Training with GFlowNets for Hierarchical Energy-Based Model Learning
proposals.motivation: Adversarial training and Energy-Based Models (EBMs) are powerful for learning complex data distributions, but they often struggle with capturing long-range dependencies and coherent multi-scale structure. Concurrently, GFlowNets have emerged as a promising framework for learning to sample from complex, compositional spaces, showing success in discrete domains like graphs. This proposal aims to unify these lines of research to address the fundamental challenge of scalable, stable learning of hierarchical representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a GFlowNet-based adversarial training framework, operating across multiple scales of abstraction, will enable more stable and sample-efficient learning of hierarchical EBMs, leading to improved generation of coherent, high-resolution structures compared to single-scale adversarial methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel adversarial training framework where a hierarchical EBM acts as the 'trainer' for a multi-scale GFlowNet 'sampler'. The GFlowNet will be structured to construct samples sequentially across scales, from low-resolution sketches to high-resolution details, with its flow matching objective conditioned on the EBM's energy at each level. The adversarial objective will be formulated as a multi-scale minimax game, where the EBM learns to assign low energy to real data and high energy to GFlowNet-generated samples at each scale. We will leverage pre-2026 architectures like U-Nets for the EBM and graph neural networks or transformers for the GFlowNet's state transition model, initially validating on synthetic hierarchical datasets and high-energy physics event generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, principled algorithm for hierarchical adversarial learning that marries the strengths of EBMs and GFlowNets. It will advance the field by providing a more stable and interpretable alternative to traditional GANs for multi-scale generation. The resulting models are expected to demonstrate superior performance in generating coherent, high-dimensional data with complex long-range dependencies.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training and GFlowNets are established separately, their integration into a unified, multi-scale learning framework is novel. The key innovation is using a GFlowNet as an adaptive, structured sampler within a hierarchical adversarial setup, moving beyond single-step or single-scale sampling mechanisms common in prior EBM and GAN literature.

proposals.title: Inverse GANs via Generative Flow Networks: Learning Energy-Guided Posteriors for Latent Code Inference
proposals.motivation: Inverse mapping from a generated sample to its latent code is a fundamental but under-explored problem in generative modeling, with applications in image editing, data compression, and understanding model representations. Current methods, like optimization-based inference or encoder training, are often inefficient, unstable, or produce poor reconstructions. A principled, probabilistic framework for this inverse problem is needed to bridge the gap between powerful GAN generators and reliable, sample-efficient inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets), trained to sample latent codes proportional to a reward defined by the generator's output probability, can provide a more effective and scalable framework for learning the posterior distribution over latent codes given an observation than existing encoder-based or optimization-based approaches.
proposals.method: We will frame the inverse problem as a sequential decision process where a GFlowNet constructs a latent code z through a series of refinements. The terminal reward for a trajectory will be defined by the energy of the generated sample, R(z) = exp(-E(G(z))), where E is an energy function (e.g., based on a discriminator or a pretrained feature model). We will leverage the GFlowNet's ability to sample from complex, high-dimensional distributions without requiring explicit likelihoods. Specifically, we will adapt the Trajectory Balance or Detailed Balance loss, conditioning the state transitions on the target observation x = G(z_target), enabling the network to learn a stochastic inverse mapping p(z | x).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, energy-based inference framework for GANs that provides a distribution over plausible latent codes, not just a point estimate. It will demonstrate how GFlowNets can be applied to inverse problems in deep generative models, extending their use beyond combinatorial spaces. The resulting method will be evaluated on standard benchmarks for reconstruction fidelity, editability, and mode coverage compared to encoder GANs and gradient-based inversion.
proposals.novelty: While GFlowNets have been used for generative modeling and combinatorial optimization, applying them to learn the posterior in a continuous, pre-trained generative model like a GAN is novel. This approach moves beyond deterministic encoders by learning to sample from the full posterior distribution p(z|x) guided by a learned energy, a non-obvious synergy of Courville's expertise in energy-based models and GFlowNets.

proposals.title: Ensemble GFlowNets: Scalable Adversarial Training via Distributed Compositional Energy Learning
proposals.motivation: Adversarial training and energy-based models (EBMs) are powerful for learning complex distributions but scale poorly with model complexity and data dimensionality. Training a single, large model in a minimax game is computationally intensive and prone to mode collapse. A distributed, ensemble-based approach could overcome these bottlenecks by decomposing the problem, aligning with the need for scalable probabilistic learning in fields like high-energy physics.
